query: effect of corticosteroids on tissue repair and healing

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Healing Phases -- Other Issues -- Wound Healing Controversies and the Future
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal tissue has been shown to heal without scars, but its clinical role has yet to be determined. Transforming growth factor and several other cytokines all have a role in healing, but when to add or how much of the cytokine is needed for adequate healing is still being debated. Hyperbaric oxygen can promote healing, but the technique and success rate are not well established. Even though there are many anecdotal reports on honey and wound healing, controlled studies show that the benefits of honey are marginal at best. Finally, medications that can adversely affect healing include anticonvulsants, steroids, antibiotics, angiogenesis inhibitors, and NSAIDs. Drugs known to promote healing include insulin, vitamins, thyroid hormone, and iron. The new frontier for wound healing is the use of stem cells, but there are ethical issues regarding the widespread use of this technology. [7] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Other Issues -- Vitamins, Trace Elements, and Growth Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Although a detailed discussion of the role of vitamins and trace elements in the WHP is well beyond the scope of the current review/synopsis of this topic, the authors feel strongly that a brief overview of this topic is fully warranted. It has long been known that Vitamin A may have beneficial effects on wound healing in the setting of previous systemic exposure to corticosteroids. More specifically, Vitamin A has been shown to overcome the inhibitory effects of cortisone (and related corticosteroids) on the rate of gain in tensile strength of the wound [34] [35] [34] . This may be especially pronounced in early stages of the WHP. At the same time, Vitamin A by itself does not seem to be a significant modulator of the WHP, suggesting that its beneficial action in the setting of prior corticosteroid use is related to the interaction(s) specific to corticosteroid-specific pathway(s) [36] [37] [36] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Wound Healing -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic Wound Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with immunosuppression are more susceptible to infections and have a diminished ability to fight infections, leading to delayed wound healing due to an extended inflammatory phase. Anti-inflammatory drugs, particularly during the first 3 days of healing; corticosteroids; immunosuppressants; and chemotherapy agents can alter wound healing. [6] Similarly, some comorbidities negatively influence wound healing, with diabetes mellitus being one of the most significant diseases associated with chronic wound formation. Poor perfusion, neuropathy, immunosuppression, slower collagen synthesis and accumulation, and inadequate angiogenesis often cause an increased risk of infection and delayed wound healing in patients with diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Clinical Significance -- Prolonged Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Possible causes of prolonged wound healing include: Diabetes mellitus Rheumatoid arthritis Vascular or arterial insufficiency Zinc deficiency Low human growth hormone levels Older age Obesity Trauma Excess moisture Tissue hypoxia Medications (steroids, nonsteroidal antiinflammatories, chemotherapy) Alcoholism Tobacco abuse

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Issues of Concern -- Factors Affecting Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Stress has been demonstrated to be a major contributor to a broad range of health conditions and illnesses, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, and obesity. Stress states lead to upregulation of stress hormones via the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and the release of adrenocortical hormones. Resultant changes include elevated levels of cortisol, glucocorticoids, and catecholamines. Cortisol works to blunt the immune response by blocking the production of important cytokines such as IL-1beta, IL-6, and TNF-alpha. The impairment of immune response ultimately leads to deficient wound healing [25] [26] .

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: 11. Dermatologic adverse effects: Corticosteroid use induces skin atrophy, leading to thinning and fragility of the skin and striae and purpura. The striae are usually permanent, while the other changes are reversible. [2] Additionally, impairment of cutaneous wound healing is associated with corticosteroid use. [2] Corticosteroids inhibit the migration of leukocytes and macrophages, reduce collagen synthesis and wound maturation, and decrease the expression of keratinocyte growth factor after a wound. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertrophic Scarring Keloids -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: First-line therapy of keloids and hypertrophic scarring includes occlusive dressings, compression therapy, and steroids. Occlusive dressings are thought to work by reducing collagen synthesis by decreased delivery of blood, oxygen, and nutrients to the scar. Patients must wear these dressings twenty-three hours a day, and they work best immediately after injury or surgery. [11] Compression therapy is another treatment option that provides local pressure to the affected area; this has a thinning effect on the skin and reduces the cohesiveness of the collagen fibers. Examples of compression therapy include button compression for earlobe keloids, pressure earrings, ACE bandages, and spandex bandages. Steroids are also first-line and can be used alone or in conjunction with other therapies. Steroids decrease the synthesis of collagen and proinflammatory mediators. Intralesional triamcinolone injections are used every four to six weeks throughout several months as either monotherapy or as an adjuvant therapy to other treatments. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Dehiscence -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Proper wound healing occurs in three general phases, allowing the damage to heal and new tissue to replace the damaged tissue. The three phases include inflammation, proliferation, and maturation. [3] [4] [5] The repaired wound can be expected to obtain 80% of the original tensile strength over two years, but will not achieve the same level of pre-injury strength. Poor wound healing can occur due to the disruption of any of the three phases of healing. Common risk factors for abnormal healing include the presence of necrotic tissue, infection, ischemia, smoking, diabetes, malnutrition, glucocorticoid use, and radiation exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Striae develop due to injury to the dermis and mechanical stress. Inflammation and edema of the dermis result in collagen deposition in the region of the mechanical stress. Striae appear histologically as scars and are permanent. [17]

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe factors that are associated with better wound healing. Identify the wounds that are likely to experience impaired healing. Summarize the recent advances in wound care and treatments available. Explain how use of an interprofessional team leads to better outcomes in the care of wounds. retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Wound healing is a vast topic area, with many aspects remaining poorly understood despite the tremendous amount of progress over the past few decades. Successful wound healing requires attention to multiple factors, incorporating nutritional, microbiologic, mechanical/coverage/dressing, and circulatory considerations. Various co-morbid conditions (e.g., diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, chronic steroid administration) and environmental factors (e.g., tobacco abuse) play important roles as modulators of the WHP. More recently, a better understanding of the role micronutrients play in wound healing, along with the introduction of novel wound care therapeutics (e.g., NPWT, MDT, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, growth factors), created a foundation for further progress in this critically important area of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Assessment -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyurea has been reported in multiple instances to cause non-healing ulcerations Chemotherapeutic drugs delay cell migration, decrease wound matrix formation, lower collagen production, impair fibroblast proliferation, and inhibit wound contraction; furthermore, they weaken immune functions, thereby impeding the natural inflammatory phase of healing, and increasing the risk of infection [15] Steroids decrease inflammation, inhibit epithelialization, and decrease collagen production [15] Vitamin A reverses delayed wound healing from steroids [39]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1876
retrieved_docs.content: Preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as cicatrants, meaning they are agents that promote tissue repair and scar formation, and are used to enhance the healing process of wounds and ulcerative lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2524
retrieved_docs.content: Dexpanthenol and trafermin are both classified as “other cicatrizants” within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system, indicating that they belong to the same therapeutic group of agents used to promote wound healing and tissue repair. This categorization highlights their shared role as cicatrizant medications, which are employed to support the healing process in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, clocortolone, diflorasone, and klobetasol are all classified as antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents typically belonging to the corticosteroid family. As antiflogistika, each of these drugs exerts potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by binding to glucocorticoid receptors, thereby inhibiting the synthesis of inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins and cytokines. Deflazacort is often used for chronic inflammatory diseases and certain autoimmune conditions, while clocortolone and diflorasone are commonly formulated for topical application to treat dermatologic inflammation. Klobetasol, one of the most potent topical corticosteroids, is employed for severe skin disorders requiring strong anti‑inflammatory action. Recognizing that all four agents share the same therapeutic class (antiflogistika) helps clinicians select the appropriate formulation and potency for specific inflammatory conditions, optimize patient outcomes, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic corticosteroid exposure.

Understanding these connections aids clinicians in recognizing the antimicrobial spectrum of chloramfenikol‑containing preparations, their placement within antibiotic stewardship frameworks, and the potential for synergistic effects when combined with corticosteroids, antifungals, or other supportive agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1408
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers, indicating that these enzymatic agents constitute a specific type of therapeutic preparation used to manage wound and ulcer conditions. This relationship highlights that enzymatic formulations belong to the broader category of wound‑and‑ulcer treatments, emphasizing their role within the spectrum of medical preparations aimed at promoting healing and tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 925
retrieved_docs.content: Other cicatrizants in ATC is a therapeutic category that includes the agents dexpantenol, becaplermin, and trafermin, each of which is considered a member of this group of wound‑healing and scar‑treatment medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.
